nodes.name: india-healthcare-access
nodes.content: India healthcare ACCESS domain (DAIS-for-Good 2026, Virtue Foundation). The 'Medical Desert Planner': join SUPPLY (10k geocoded facilities) against DEMAND (NFHS-5 district health burden) through the India Post PIN crosswalk to find where the highest-risk gaps in care are. One Genie space backs all three tables. Honesty contract: the facility table is a ~10k SAMPLE, never a census — facility counts are dataset COVERAGE, not absolute supply; cite the source text for any facility claim; communicate uncertainty.

props.summary: India healthcare access — supply (facilities) x demand (NFHS-5 burden) x geo (PIN) to map medical deserts.

nodes.name: India Healthcare Access Space
nodes.content: Genie space over the three Virtue Foundation tables. Ask natural-language questions; it writes Spark SQL over `databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026`.`virtue_foundation_dataset`.*. Tables: facilities (supply), nfhs_5_district_health_indicators (demand burden), india_post_pincode_directory (PIN->district->state crosswalk). Use for ANY question about facility supply, district health burden, or access gaps / medical deserts that join across them. The facility<->district bridge is facilities.address_zipOrPostcode (a 6-digit PIN) -> pincode dir (modal district per pincode) -> NFHS district_name.

props.when_to_use: India healthcare: facility supply/composition/trust, NFHS-5 district health burden, PIN geography, and medical-desert (supply x demand) joins.
props.summary: One Genie space over facilities + NFHS-5 + PIN directory.

nodes.name: databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities
nodes.content: Table `databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities` — 10,088 rows; 10,000 are address_country='India' (FILTER to India; 88 junk rows are CSV-misparsed). SUPPLY side. One row per facility (unique_id; 11 dup ids + cluster_id groups). 99.7% geocoded (latitude/longitude doubles). facilityTypeId: hospital 5,637 / clinic 3,782 / dentist 490. operatorTypeId ~88% private. Rich self-reported free-text: specialties / procedure / equipment / capability (~98% present) — these are SCRAPED CLAIMS, not verified. Thin proof: numberDoctors 36% / capacity 25% / yearEstablished 48% / recency_of_page_update 35%. address_zipOrPostcode holds a 6-digit PIN (the join bridge to district).

props.summary: 10,000 India healthcare facilities (supply): type, operator, geocode, uneven free-text claims.

nodes.name: databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.nfhs_5_district_health_indicators
nodes.content: Table `databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.nfhs_5_district_health_indicators` — 706 rows (698 distinct districts; some district names repeat across states). DEMAND side: NFHS-5 (field period 2019-21) district fact sheets, 107 health indicators across household conditions, maternal/reproductive health, child health & vaccination, nutrition, anaemia, NCDs, cancer screening, tobacco/alcohol. Grain = district (district_name + state_ut). ~24 indicator columns are STRING-but-numeric (CAST before aggregating); '*' = suppressed (NULL, not 0); '(29.5)' = estimate from 25-49 unweighted cases. Massive inequality across districts.

props.summary: NFHS-5 (2019-21) district health burden: anaemia, stunting, births, vaccination, insurance, NCDs.

nodes.name: databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.india_post_pincode_directory
nodes.content: Table `databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.india_post_pincode_directory` — 165,627 rows. India Post PIN-code directory: the geo CROSSWALK that bridges a facility's PIN to an NFHS district. Grain = POST OFFICE, not PIN: a single pincode fans out to many rows and can map to >1 district — DEDUP to the MODAL district per pincode before joining (a raw join on pincode fans rows out). 19,586 unique PINs, 750 districts, 37 states. latitude/longitude are strings with ~12,600 'NA' — not every post office is geocoded.

props.summary: India Post PIN->district->state crosswalk; the facility-PIN to NFHS-district bridge (dedup to modal district).

props.notes: 6-digit PIN. Join key from facility.address_zipOrPostcode. NOT unique (post-office grain) — dedup to modal district.

nodes.name: facilities_total
nodes.content: Count of India facilities = COUNT over facilities WHERE address_country='India' (10,000 rows; 88 junk rows excluded). DEDUP first: 11 unique_id values repeat as byte-identical rows -> 9,989 DISTINCT facilities. DATASET COVERAGE, not census (FDR lists 47k+).

props.definition: In-dataset India facility count (9,989 distinct after dedup)

nodes.name: private_share
nodes.content: Private operator share = private / (operators excluding the literal 'null' string). 88.4% of all India rows / 94.9% of the 9,313 with a known operator. Public+government just 4.7%.

nodes.name: facility_geocoding_rate
nodes.content: Share of facilities with both latitude & longitude = 99.7% (9,970/10,000); 99.64% inside India's bbox. Clean doubles — map directly, no geocoding step.

props.definition: Share of facilities with usable lat/lon
props.formula: AVG(latitude IS NOT NULL AND longitude IS NOT NULL)

nodes.name: facility_supply_concentration
nodes.content: Across-state Gini of facility coverage = 0.609 (top-5 states 49.8%, top-10 73.7%). Concentration of DATASET COVERAGE, not real supply.

nodes.name: health_burden_index
nodes.content: HBI = mean of per-district percentile ranks (0=lowest burden, 50=median, 100=highest) across 10 NFHS-5 indicators: anaemia (women+child), stunting, wasting, underweight, and the inverse of institutional birth, 4+ANC, full immunization, insurance, improved sanitation. RELATIVE rank, NOT a clinical prevalence.

nodes.name: women_anaemia_rate
nodes.content: all_w15_49_who_are_anaemic_pct. Median district 57.2%, range 14.9% (Kohima) to 93.5% (Leh/Ladakh). Over half of districts have a majority of women anaemic.

nodes.name: child_stunting_rate
nodes.content: child_u5_who_are_stunted_height_for_age_18_pct (STRING->CAST). Median 32.8%, range 13.2% (Jagatsinghapur) to 60.6% (Pashchimi Singhbhum, also worst on underweight 62.4%).

nodes.name: institutional_birth_rate
nodes.content: institutional_birth_5y_pct. Median 92.2%, range 21.4% (Mon, Nagaland) to 100% (South Goa). Care-access proxy.

nodes.name: full_immunization_rate
nodes.content: child_12_23m_fully_vaccinated... (STRING->CAST). Median 85.0%, min 45.0% (Ukhrul). Heavy suppression — 22 '*' districts + 323 parenthesized estimates: treat tail as low-confidence.

nodes.name: health_insurance_coverage
nodes.content: hh_member_covered_health_insurance_pct. Range 1.2% (South Andaman) to 97.8% (Barmer) = 81.5x spread — the widest 'care lottery' indicator.

nodes.name: facilities_per_district
nodes.content: Count of dataset facilities resolved to an NFHS district via the PIN crosswalk. Mean 11.6, median 2, max 325 (Pune). 245 districts = 0. COVERAGE, not supply.

props.definition: In-dataset facility count per NFHS district

nodes.name: desert_score
nodes.content: Desert score = high health_burden_index x low facilities_per_district. Ranks high-need + few/zero-coverage districts. Top-15 led by Araria (Bihar, 0 facilities, score 3.73).

props.definition: Composite of demand burden and supply scarcity

nodes.name: burden_supply_correlation
nodes.content: Spearman correlation of district burden vs facility coverage = NEGATIVE, rho ~ -0.2 to -0.27 (p<1e-8). High-burden tertile averages 5.5 facilities vs ~14.6 for the rest — supply does NOT follow need.

props.definition: Correlation between health burden and facility coverage

nodes.name: facility_trust_score
nodes.content: Transparent additive 0-100 score over 8 corroboration signals: recent_update 25, has_doctors 20, has_website 15, has_capacity 15, has_phone 10, affiliated_staff 5, social_2plus 5, rich_desc 5. Median facility scores just 45; 50.9% below 50. Auditable analyst design, NOT learned, NOT real-world quality.

nodes.name: unverifiable_claim_rate
nodes.content: Share of facilities making an advanced-capability claim (MRI/CT/ICU/cancer/cardiac/dialysis/NICU/transplant in equipment/capability text) with NO doctor count, NO capacity, AND no recent update = 1,219 (12.2% of all India, 29.9% of the 4,077 advanced-claimers).

props.definition: Advanced claims with zero hard corroboration
props.formula: COUNT(advanced_claim AND no_doctors AND no_capacity AND stale) / COUNT(*)

nodes.name: R-india-filter
nodes.content: Always filter facilities to address_country='India' (= address_countryCode='IN'); 88 of 10,088 rows are CSV-misparse junk (coordinates/JSON/free-text/'kie'/None in the country column). Leaves exactly 10,000 analyzable rows.

props.statement: Filter address_country='India' on facilities; 88 junk rows otherwise.
props.rationale: Misparsed CSV rows carry coordinates/JSON in the country field and pollute every facility metric.

nodes.name: R-facility-dedup
nodes.content: facilities.unique_id is NOT unique: 11 IDs each appear twice as BYTE-IDENTICAL rows (confirmed exact dupes, also flagged by cluster_id's 11 size-2 clusters). SELECT DISTINCT unique_id (or drop_duplicates) collapses 10,000 -> 9,989 facilities. Safe — exact re-scrapes, no merge logic needed; do it before any facility COUNT or rate so totals aren't inflated by ~0.1%.

props.statement: facilities.unique_id repeats 11x as exact-duplicate rows — dedup (DISTINCT unique_id) to 9,989 before counting.
props.rationale: 11 byte-identical re-scrapes inflate every facility count if not deduped; cluster_id confirms they are the same physical site.

nodes.name: R-coverage-not-census
nodes.content: THE honesty contract. The facilities table is a ~10k web-discovered SAMPLE, not a census (India's FDR lists 47k+). Every facility count / desert / gap claim must be framed as in-dataset COVERAGE, never absolute supply. A zero-facility district = zero in THIS dataset (a coverage/outreach gap), NOT 'no care exists'. NO per-capita anywhere (no population column in the 3 tables). Cite the source TEXT field for any facility capability claim. Communicating this uncertainty is itself scored by the rubric.

props.statement: Facility counts are dataset coverage, not supply; no per-capita; cite source text; state uncertainty.
props.rationale: Presenting a sample coverage gap as proven absence of care is the central failure mode the 'Medical Desert Planner' track tests.

nodes.name: R-string-null-sentinel
nodes.content: Missing values in facilities are the literal string 'null' (also 'none'/'NA'/''), NOT SQL NULL. Strip these before any fill-rate, presence, or aggregation. numberDoctors looks 99.7% populated but is real for only 36.2%; capacity 25.0%; recency 35.4%. A naive COUNT(col) overstates evidence ~3x.

props.statement: Treat literal 'null'/'none'/'NA'/'' as missing before counting or aggregating facility fields.
props.rationale: The dataset encodes missing as a string sentinel; SQL NULL checks miss it entirely.

nodes.name: R-cast-facility-numeric
nodes.content: facilities numberDoctors, capacity, address_zipOrPostcode, distinct_social_media_presence_count and number_of_facts_about_the_organization are STRING-but-numeric. Strip ()/* then try_cast(... AS DOUBLE) (errors->NULL), and require value>0 before treating as evidence.

props.statement: CAST facility string-numeric cols (strip ()*, try_cast) and require >0 before use.
props.rationale: Aggregating a STRING column errors or sorts lexically; the sentinel 'null' also poisons casts.

nodes.name: R-normalize-state
nodes.content: address_stateOrRegion is polluted: 234 distinct values (city names + spelling variants + junk) vs 36 real states/UTs. Normalize UPPER/strip/[^A-Z0-9 &]->space + an alias map (Tamilnadu->Tamil Nadu, Orissa->Odisha, UP/U P->Uttar Pradesh, Chattisgarh->Chhattisgarh; city names->drop) before any state rollup. 96.9% map cleanly.

props.statement: Normalize + alias-map address_stateOrRegion before any state-level aggregation.
props.rationale: Raw state strings double-count and scatter facilities across 234 spurious categories.

nodes.name: R-operatortype-null-string
nodes.content: operatorTypeId contains a literal STRING 'null' (687 India rows) distinct from real NULL. Replace 'null'->missing before computing private/public share, or 'null' is counted as its own operator category.

props.statement: Replace literal 'null' in operatorTypeId before computing operator shares.
props.rationale: Otherwise the missing sentinel ranks as the 3rd-largest 'operator type'.

nodes.name: R-drop-constant-orgtype
nodes.content: organization_type is the constant 'facility' for all 10,000 India rows — no analytical signal. Do not group or facet by it.

props.statement: organization_type is constant 'facility' — never group by it.
props.rationale: A constant column adds noise and a useless facet.

nodes.name: R-claims-self-reported
nodes.content: Every facility attribute (specialties, equipment, capability, procedure, description, numberDoctors, capacity) is self-reported SCRAPED free text. Never present a claim as verified fact; cite the source field name when stating a capability. A 'null' proof field = undisclosed, not proof the capability is fake (risk-to-verify, not misrepresentation).

props.statement: Facility claims are self-reported scraped text — cite the field, never assert as verified.
props.rationale: The Trust/Referral use cases require citing evidence and communicating uncertainty, not trusting scraped claims.

nodes.name: R-recency-date-caveat
nodes.content: recency_of_page_update spans 2003-2027 and contains future-dated stamps (scrape artifacts). Parse with errors->coerce; treat a date>today as 'recent' rather than dropping it; disclose the parse rule when reporting recency-based trust.

props.statement: recency_of_page_update has future-dated scrape artifacts — coerce-parse and disclose.
props.rationale: Dropping future dates silently discards real (if odd) freshness signals.

nodes.name: R-nfhs-cast
nodes.content: 49 of 107 NFHS-5 indicator columns are stored STRING-but-numeric. Always CAST after stripping ()/* and whitespace: pd.to_numeric(s.str.replace(r'[()*]','',regex=True).str.strip(),errors='coerce') or try_cast(regexp_replace(col,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE). Casting raw fails or zeroes out.

props.statement: CAST the 49 string-numeric NFHS columns (strip ()*) before aggregating.
props.rationale: Direct aggregation of a STRING percentage column errors or sorts lexically.

nodes.name: R-nfhs-suppressed-star
nodes.content: A '*' in any NFHS-5 cell means the estimate was suppressed (sample too small). Treat as NULL, exclude from means/counts — NEVER as zero (zero fabricates 'perfect'/'zero-burden' districts). 4,125 cells across 29 columns are suppressed.

props.statement: NFHS '*' = suppressed = NULL, never zero.
props.rationale: Coercing '*' to 0 invents perfect or zero-burden districts.

nodes.name: R-nfhs-parenthesized-estimate
nodes.content: A parenthesized value like '(29.5)' is an estimate from 25-49 unweighted cases. Strip the parens to use it, but FLAG it low-confidence and never quote it as a headline. 5,068 cells across 48 columns are parenthesized (full immunization has 323).

props.statement: NFHS '(x.x)' = low-sample estimate — usable but never a headline.
props.rationale: 25-49 unweighted cases give wide confidence intervals; tail districts are unreliable.

nodes.name: R-nfhs5-vintage
nodes.content: NFHS-5 fieldwork was 2019-21. Always label these district indicators as the 2019-21 baseline, never current-year/2024. This vintage pre-dates the facilities supply table; NFHS-6 (2023-24) is separate and not directly comparable.

props.statement: Label NFHS-5 indicators as the 2019-21 baseline, not current.
props.rationale: Mixing NFHS-5 demand with later supply or treating it as 'now' misstates the timeline.

nodes.name: R-hbi-relative-percentile
nodes.content: health_burden_index is a RELATIVE percentile-rank composite (0=lowest burden, 50=median, 100=highest), not a prevalence. Always describe it as a relative ranking and state its 10 component indicators and the inversion of the 5 higher-is-better ones.

props.statement: HBI is a relative percentile composite — describe as ranking, list components.
props.rationale: Readers misread a 78 as '78% of people' instead of '78th-percentile burden district'.

nodes.name: R-demand-not-supply
nodes.content: NFHS-5 indicators measure health NEED (demand) only — they say nothing about facility supply. A 'medical desert' claim REQUIRES joining high burden to LOW in-dataset facility coverage; and facility counts are dataset coverage (a ~10k sample), never a census.

props.statement: NFHS = demand only; 'desert' needs the burden x low-coverage join.
props.rationale: High burden alone is need, not a desert; a desert is need unmet by supply.

nodes.name: R-pin-fanout-dedup
nodes.content: india_post_pincode_directory is post-office grain (8.5 rows/PIN; 165,627 rows / 19,586 PINs) and 1,478 PINs (7.55%) fan out to >1 district (max 4). DEDUP to the MODAL (district, statename) per pincode before joining facilities, or facility counts inflate ~8x. Its 'NA'-string coords (7.25%) cast to NULL silently.

props.statement: Dedup pincode dir to the modal (district,state) per PIN before joining; raw join fans out ~8x.
props.rationale: A raw pincode join multiplies every facility into ~8 post-office rows.

nodes.name: R-district-name-normalization
nodes.content: Normalize district names UPPER->trim->collapse whitespace->drop non-[A-Z0-9 &] on BOTH sides of the facility<->NFHS join. Recovers 81.2% of India facilities; the ~19% miss is spelling/transliteration drift (a true spatial point-in-polygon join would recover more). 704/706 NFHS names carry trailing whitespace.

props.statement: Normalize (UPPER/trim/collapse) district names both sides before joining; 81.2% recovery.
props.rationale: Trailing whitespace and transliteration variants make exact-string joins silently fail.

nodes.name: R-district-state-collision
nodes.content: Name-only district join collides 8 normalized names across states (Aurangabad=Bihar+Maharashtra, Bijapur=Chhattisgarh+Karnataka, Balrampur, Bilaspur, Chandel, Hamirpur, Pratapgarh, Raigarh). Summing on district name alone double-counts these (+85 rows). Key on (normalized state, district) or do a spatial join.

props.statement: Key district joins on (state, district) — 8 names collide across states.
props.rationale: Joining on district name alone double-counts the 8 cross-state homonyms.

nodes.name: facilities-clean-india-count
nodes.content:
When to use: how many facilities (and how clean) — the dataset universe.

```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total,
       COUNT_IF(address_country='India') AS india_rows,
       COUNT_IF(address_country<>'India' OR address_country IS NULL) AS junk_rows,
       COUNT(DISTINCT unique_id) AS distinct_ids
FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities;
-- 10,088 total -> 10,000 India, 88 junk, 9,989 distinct (11 dup ids)
```

props.question: How many facilities are in the India dataset and how clean is it?
props.notes: Honor R-india-filter; counts are dataset coverage (R-coverage-not-census).

nodes.name: facility-composition-by-type-operator
nodes.content:
When to use: facility type & operator mix.

```sql
SELECT facilityTypeId, COUNT(*) AS n
FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities
WHERE address_country='India' GROUP BY facilityTypeId ORDER BY n DESC;
-- hospital 5,637 / clinic 3,782 / dentist 490
SELECT CASE WHEN operatorTypeId='null' THEN NULL ELSE operatorTypeId END AS operator, COUNT(*) AS n
FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities
WHERE address_country='India' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY n DESC;
-- private 8,842 (88.4%) vs public+government 471 (4.7%)
```

props.question: What kinds of facilities are these and who operates them?
props.notes: Replace literal 'null' (R-operatortype-null-string); don't facet organization_type (R-drop-constant-orgtype).

nodes.name: facility-supply-concentration-by-state
nodes.content:
When to use: how concentrated is facility coverage across states.

```sql
-- Normalize state upstream (R-normalize-state). Top states + share:
SELECT address_stateOrRegion AS state, COUNT(*) AS facilities,
       ROUND(100.0*COUNT(*)/SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (), 1) AS pct
FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities
WHERE address_country='India'
GROUP BY address_stateOrRegion ORDER BY facilities DESC LIMIT 10;
-- Maharashtra 1,575 (16.3%), Gujarat 981, UP 933, TN 802, Karnataka 529; top-5=49.8%, top-10=73.7%, Gini 0.609
```

props.question: How concentrated is facility coverage across Indian states?
props.notes: Concentration of dataset coverage, not real supply (R-coverage-not-census).

nodes.name: nfhs-burden-cast-preamble
nodes.content:
When to use: ANY NFHS-5 indicator aggregation — the CAST/suppression preamble.

```sql
-- '*' = suppressed (NULL not 0); '(x.x)' = low-sample estimate; many cols are STRING.
SELECT district_name, state_ut,
       try_cast(regexp_replace(child_u5_who_are_stunted_height_for_age_18_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS stunting_pct,
       try_cast(regexp_replace(child_6_59m_who_are_anaemic_lt_11_0_g_dl_22_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS child_anaemia_pct
FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.nfhs_5_district_health_indicators;
-- try_cast returns NULL for '*'/garbage; never coerce '*' to 0
```

props.question: How do I correctly aggregate NFHS-5 indicators given the CAST/suppression gotchas?
props.notes: Honors R-nfhs-cast, R-nfhs-suppressed-star, R-nfhs-parenthesized-estimate.

nodes.name: nfhs-worst-burden-districts
nodes.content:
When to use: which districts have the highest health need (demand side).

```sql
-- Per-indicator percentile ranks -> composite HBI (see health_burden_index). Single-indicator example:
SELECT district_name, state_ut,
       try_cast(regexp_replace(child_6_59m_who_are_anaemic_lt_11_0_g_dl_22_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS child_anaemia
FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.nfhs_5_district_health_indicators
WHERE child_6_59m_who_are_anaemic_lt_11_0_g_dl_22_pct <> '*'
ORDER BY child_anaemia DESC LIMIT 15;
-- HBI top: Araria 89.7, Lakhisarai 88.0, Nalanda 86.4; 10 of 15 worst are Bihar
```

props.question: Which districts have the highest overall health burden / greatest need?
props.notes: HBI is a relative percentile composite (R-hbi-relative-percentile); demand only (R-demand-not-supply).

nodes.name: nfhs-state-burden-rollup
nodes.content:
When to use: state-level health burden (key on TRIM district+state).

```sql
SELECT TRIM(state_ut) AS state, COUNT(*) AS districts,
       ROUND(AVG(<district HBI>), 1) AS mean_burden
FROM ( /* district HBI subquery */ )
GROUP BY TRIM(state_ut) HAVING COUNT(*)>=3 ORDER BY mean_burden DESC;
-- Bihar 78.4 (n=38) vs Kerala 16.9 (n=14): a 61.5-pt gulf (4.6x). Jharkhand 69.8, UP 61.6 worst after Bihar.
```

props.question: Which states have the highest district health burden?
props.notes: Key on (TRIM state, district) — R-district-state-collision; 'Maharastra' misspelling in source.

nodes.name: care-lottery-spread
nodes.content:
When to use: how unequal is access across districts (the 'care lottery').

```sql
SELECT 'health_insurance' AS indicator, MIN(v) AS min_pct, MAX(v) AS max_pct, ROUND(MAX(v)/NULLIF(MIN(v),0),1) AS spread_x
FROM (SELECT try_cast(hh_member_covered_health_insurance_pct AS DOUBLE) AS v
      FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.nfhs_5_district_health_indicators) WHERE v IS NOT NULL;
-- insurance 1.2->97.8 (81.5x); 4+ANC 4.4->98.7 (22.4x); women anaemia 14.9->93.5 (6.3x); institutional birth 21.4->100 (4.7x)
```

props.question: How unequal is health access across districts?
props.notes: Report p10-p90 alongside min-max (single extreme districts are small UTs).

nodes.name: facility-to-district-join
nodes.content:
When to use: the SUPPLY<->DEMAND bridge — resolve facilities to an NFHS district (THE join).

```sql
WITH pin_modal AS (  -- dedup post-office grain to modal (district,state) per PIN (R-pin-fanout-dedup)
  SELECT pincode,
         FIRST(district) OVER (PARTITION BY pincode ORDER BY cnt DESC) AS district,
         FIRST(statename) OVER (PARTITION BY pincode ORDER BY cnt DESC) AS statename
  FROM (SELECT pincode, district, statename, COUNT(*) cnt
        FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.india_post_pincode_directory
        GROUP BY pincode, district, statename)),
fac AS (
  SELECT unique_id, CAST(regexp_extract(address_zipOrPostcode,'([0-9]{6})',1) AS INT) AS pin
  FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities
  WHERE address_country='India')
SELECT f.unique_id, upper(trim(p.district)) AS district_norm, p.statename
FROM fac f LEFT JOIN pin_modal p ON f.pin = p.pincode;
-- coverage: 97.7% parseable PIN -> 95.7% PIN-district -> 81.2% match an NFHS district by normalized name
```

props.question: How do I join facilities to NFHS districts (supply to demand)?
props.notes: Honors R-pin-fanout-dedup, R-district-name-normalization, R-district-state-collision; join ceiling 81.2%.

nodes.name: zero-facility-districts
nodes.content:
When to use: the medical-desert COVERAGE GAP headline.

```sql
-- facilities resolved to district (facility-to-district-join) RIGHT JOINed to all NFHS districts
SELECT n.district_name, n.state_ut, COUNT(f.unique_id) AS facilities
FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.nfhs_5_district_health_indicators n
LEFT JOIN resolved_facilities f ON upper(trim(n.district_name)) = f.district_norm
GROUP BY n.district_name, n.state_ut;
-- ~245 of 698 districts (35%) have ZERO facilities in this dataset; mean 11.6, median 2, max 325 (Pune)
```

props.question: How many districts have no facilities in the dataset (coverage gaps)?
props.notes: Zero = zero in THIS sample, not absence of care (R-coverage-not-census).

nodes.name: desert-score-ranking
nodes.content:
When to use: rank the priority medical-desert districts (high burden x low coverage).

```sql
SELECT district, state, burden_index, facilities,
       (zscore(burden_index) - zscore(ln(1+facilities))) AS desert_score
FROM district_burden_supply
ORDER BY desert_score DESC LIMIT 15;
-- Araria(Bihar,0 fac,3.73), Pakur(JH,0), Lakhisarai(Bihar,0), Jamui(Bihar,0), Sahibganj(JH,0); 13/15 Bihar+Jharkhand, 13 zero-facility
```

props.question: Which high-need districts should a planner prioritise as medical deserts?
props.notes: desert_score combines demand + supply; coverage caveat applies.

nodes.name: burden-supply-correlation
nodes.content:
When to use: does facility supply follow need? (the analytical twist).

```sql
-- correlate district burden vs facility coverage; tertile means
SELECT corr(burden_index, facilities) AS pearson,  -- use spearman on ranks in code
       AVG(CASE WHEN burden_tertile='high' THEN facilities END) AS high_burden_mean,
       AVG(CASE WHEN burden_tertile<>'high' THEN facilities END) AS rest_mean
FROM district_burden_supply;
-- Spearman rho ~ -0.2 to -0.27 (p<1e-8, NEGATIVE); high-burden tertile 5.5 facilities vs ~14.6 for the rest
```

props.question: Does facility supply follow health need across districts?
props.notes: Mismatch is descriptive evidence of inequity, not causal/discrimination.

nodes.name: coverage-concentration-gini
nodes.content:
When to use: how concentrated is facility coverage across districts.

```sql
-- Gini + top-k share of facilities-per-district
SELECT district, facilities FROM district_facility_counts ORDER BY facilities DESC;
-- Gini 0.821; top-10 districts (Pune 325, Ahmadabad 320, Mumbai Suburban 273, Chennai 272 ...) = 28.3%; top-50 = 63.8%
```

props.question: How concentrated is facility coverage across districts?
props.notes: Concentration of dataset coverage; compounds rural/tribal gaps.

nodes.name: facility-trust-score
nodes.content:
When to use: score how well a facility's claims are corroborated.

```sql
WITH s AS (SELECT *,
  CASE WHEN try_cast(numberDoctors AS DOUBLE)>0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS has_doctors,
  CASE WHEN try_cast(capacity AS DOUBLE)>0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS has_capacity,
  CASE WHEN officialWebsite NOT IN ('null','') AND officialWebsite IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS has_website
  FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India')
SELECT unique_id, name,
  25*recent + 20*has_doctors + 15*has_website + 15*has_capacity + 10*has_phone + 5*affiliated + 5*social2 + 5*rich_desc AS trust_score
FROM s;
-- median 45/100; 50.9% below 50 (hard-proof signals are thin). Strip literal 'null' first (R-string-null-sentinel).
```

props.question: How trustworthy / well-corroborated is a facility's listing?
props.notes: Score = data corroboration, not care quality; auditable weights (R-claims-self-reported).

nodes.name: unverifiable-advanced-claims
nodes.content:
When to use: find facilities claiming advanced care they can't back up.

```sql
SELECT unique_id, name, equipment, capability
FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities
WHERE address_country='India'
  AND (lower(equipment) RLIKE '\\b(mri|ct scan|icu|cancer|oncolog|cardiac|cathlab|dialysis|nicu|transplant)\\b'
    OR lower(capability) RLIKE '\\b(mri|ct|icu|cancer|cardiac|dialysis|nicu|transplant)\\b')
  AND (numberDoctors='null' OR numberDoctors IS NULL)
  AND (capacity='null' OR capacity IS NULL);
-- 1,219 facilities (12.2% of all India, 29.9% of the 4,077 advanced-claimers) make advanced claims with zero hard proof
```

props.question: Which facilities make advanced-care claims they cannot back up?
props.notes: Word-boundary regex; 'null' proof = undisclosed, treat as risk-to-verify (R-claims-self-reported).

nodes.name: the-care-lottery
nodes.content: Within ONE country, a child's healthcare odds are decided by district, not by need. Health-insurance coverage ranges 1.2% to 97.8% across districts (81.5x), 4+ antenatal visits 4.4% to 98.7% (22.4x), women's anaemia 14.9% to 93.5% (6.3x), institutional birth 21.4% to 100% (4.7x). Where you're born dominates your access. The master narrative — 'same country, opposite odds.'

props.claim: District health access varies up to 81.5x within India — where you live decides your odds.
props.why: Outcomes track district of birth, not individual need — the structural 'care lottery'.

nodes.name: medical-deserts-zero-coverage
nodes.content: About 245 of 698 NFHS districts (35%) have ZERO facilities in this dataset — and the worst-need places are the emptiest. The five highest child-anaemia districts (Leh/Ladakh 95.5%, Sukma 91.4%, Lahul & Spiti 91.0%, Dantewada 89.9%, Tawang 89.6%) ALL have zero dataset facilities. Framed honestly as a data-COVERAGE gap, which is itself the signal: the highest-need places are invisible to data-driven planning.

props.claim: ~245 of 698 districts (35%) have zero facilities in this dataset; the 5 worst child-anaemia districts all have zero.
props.evidence: PIN-crosswalk join; 244-245 zero-coverage districts; Leh/Ladakh 95.5%, Sukma 91.4%, Lahul&Spiti 91.0%, Dantewada 89.9%, Tawang 89.6% all 0 facilities
props.why: Web-scraped sample is biased to urban/private orgs, so the highest-need rural/tribal districts are absent.
props.window: facilities sample (~10k) joined to NFHS-5 districts

nodes.name: supply-does-not-follow-need
nodes.content: The analytical twist: you'd expect facilities to follow burden, but they don't. Burden vs dataset facility coverage is significantly NEGATIVELY correlated (Spearman rho ~ -0.2 to -0.27, p<1e-8). The highest-burden tertile averages just 5.5 facilities/district vs ~14.6 for the rest; zero-facility share rises from 28.8% (low burden) to 40.9% (high burden). A descriptive mismatch between need and coverage — not proof of cause.

props.claim: Health burden and facility coverage are negatively correlated (rho ~ -0.2, p<1e-8): supply does not follow need.
props.evidence: Spearman rho -0.2..-0.27 (p<1e-8, n=706); high-burden tertile 5.5 facilities vs ~14.6; zero-fac share 28.8%->40.9%
props.why: Coverage concentrates in metros; the rural high-burden districts are under-sampled — inequity in who the data even sees.
props.window: district burden index vs in-dataset facility coverage

nodes.name: top-desert-districts-bihar-jharkhand
nodes.content: The priority deserts cluster in Bihar and Jharkhand. The 15 highest desert-score districts (high burden, <=2 facilities) are led by Araria (Bihar; 0 facilities; child anaemia 75.8%; only 25.8% of mothers get 4+ ANC visits), Pakur, Lakhisarai, Jamui and Sahibganj — 13 of 15 are in Bihar/Jharkhand and 13 have zero dataset facilities.

props.claim: 13 of the 15 worst medical-desert districts are in Bihar/Jharkhand; 13 have zero facilities.
props.evidence: desert_score top-15: Araria(Bihar,0,3.73), Pakur(JH,0), Lakhisarai(Bihar,0), Jamui(Bihar,0), Sahibganj(JH,0)...
props.why: Bihar/Jharkhand combine the highest health burden with the thinnest dataset coverage.
props.window: desert_score ranking over matched NFHS districts

nodes.name: the-burden-belt
nodes.content: Need concentrates in the east-central belt. On the 10-indicator HBI, Araria (Bihar) is the single highest-burden district at 89.7/100, and 10 of the 15 worst are in Bihar; rolled to state, Bihar averages 78.4 vs Kerala 16.9 — a 61.5-point gulf (4.6x). Jharkhand (69.8) and Uttar Pradesh (61.6) follow.

props.claim: Bihar is India's highest-burden state (HBI 78.4) vs Kerala's 16.9 — a 4.6x gulf; Araria is the worst district (89.7).
props.evidence: HBI: Araria 89.7 (top of 706), 10 of 15 worst in Bihar; state means Bihar 78.4 (n=38), Jharkhand 69.8, UP 61.6, Kerala 16.9 (n=14)
props.why: Compounding deprivation (anaemia+stunting+low ANC+low insurance) clusters in the eastern Gangetic plain.

nodes.name: the-southern-advantage
nodes.content: The mirror image: the 15 lowest-burden districts in India are entirely in the deep south — Kanniyakumari (Tamil Nadu) lowest at HBI 9.4, then a wall of Kerala districts (Kozhikode 10.7, Pathanamthitta 11.3, Thrissur 12.0). A best-15 sweep by one region is itself the inequality story.

props.claim: The 15 lowest-burden districts are all in the deep south; Kanniyakumari is lowest (HBI 9.4).
props.evidence: HBI best-15: Kanniyakumari(TN) 9.4, Kozhikode(KL) 10.7, Pathanamthitta(KL) 11.3, Thrissur(KL) 12.0, Kasaragod 12.4...
props.why: Kerala/Tamil Nadu's decades of health-system investment show up as a southern wall at the good end.

nodes.name: women-anaemia-majority
nodes.content: Anaemia among women 15-49 has a median of 57.2% — over half of all districts have a MAJORITY of women anaemic — ranging 14.9% (Kohima, Nagaland) to 93.5% (Leh/Ladakh). Child anaemia is worse (median 67.7%, max 95.5%, also Leh/Ladakh).

props.claim: The median district has 57% of women anaemic; child anaemia medians 68% and peaks at 95.5%.
props.evidence: women anaemia n=706: min 14.9 (Kohima), median 57.2, max 93.5 (Leh/Ladakh); child anaemia median 67.7, max 95.5
props.why: Anaemia is the most widespread NFHS burden — a majority-of-women crisis, not a tail problem.

nodes.name: child-malnutrition-stunting
nodes.content: The median district stunts a third of its young children (32.8%), ranging 13.2% (Jagatsinghapur, Odisha) to 60.6% (Pashchimi Singhbhum, Jharkhand — also worst on underweight at 62.4%). Stunting/wasting/underweight share worst districts, so they are not independent signals.

props.claim: Under-5 stunting medians 32.8% and peaks at 60.6% (Pashchimi Singhbhum, Jharkhand).
props.evidence: stunting n=706: 13.2 (Jagatsinghapur) -> 60.6 (Pashchimi Singhbhum), median 32.8; underweight max 62.4 (same district)
props.why: Chronic child undernutrition concentrates in the same eastern tribal belt as anaemia.

nodes.name: private-skew-public-gap
nodes.content: The supply we can see is overwhelmingly private. 88.4% of India facilities are private vs 4.7% public/government (an 18.8:1 ratio of known operators), and 94.2% are hospitals or clinics. A planner relying on this dataset sees mostly the private market, so public-sector gaps look worse than reality.

props.claim: 88% of dataset facilities are private vs 5% public — an 18.8:1 skew that hides public supply.
props.evidence: operatorTypeId (known): private 8,842 (94.9% of 9,313 known), public+government 471; facilityTypeId hospital 56.4% + clinic 37.8%
props.why: Web-scraped discovery favours private orgs with a digital footprint, undercounting public clinics.

nodes.name: supply-concentration
nodes.content: Coverage is hoarded by a handful of states and metros. The top-5 states hold 49.8% of dataset facilities (Maharashtra alone 16.3%) and the top-10 hold 73.7% (across-state Gini 0.609); across districts the Gini is 0.821, with the top-10 districts (Pune 325, Ahmadabad 320, Mumbai Suburban 273, Chennai 272...) holding 28.3% and the top-50 holding 63.8%. The northeast is nearly absent (Sikkim 4, Arunachal 3, Mizoram few).

props.claim: Top-5 states hold ~50% of dataset facilities; district coverage Gini is 0.821 (top-50 districts = 63.8%).
props.evidence: state Gini 0.609 (top-5 49.8%, Maharashtra 16.3%, top-10 73.7%); district Gini 0.821 (top-10 28.3%, top-50 63.8%)
props.why: Dataset coverage concentrates in dense urban/private markets, compounding rural/tribal blind spots.

nodes.name: claims-rich-proof-thin
nodes.content: Facilities say a lot and prove little. Claim text is near-complete (description 100%, specialties 99.7%, capability 99.4%) but hard proof is sparse: numberDoctors real for only 36.3%, capacity 25.2%, recency 35.4% (the raw 99.7% is the literal-'null' sentinel). The median facility scores just 45/100 on the trust score; 50.9% fall below 50.

props.claim: Facility claims are ~99% present but hard proof (doctors 36%, capacity 25%) is thin; median trust 45/100.
props.evidence: present: specialties 99.7%, capability 99.4%; real-fill: numberDoctors 36.3%, capacity 25.2%, recency 35.4%; trust median 45, 50.9% below 50
props.why: The data is scraped self-report — believability, not capability; a planner must verify before acting.

nodes.name: unverifiable-advanced-claims
nodes.content: 1,219 facilities (12.2% of all India, 29.9% of the 4,077 making advanced-capability claims) advertise MRI/CT/ICU/cancer/cardiac/dialysis/NICU/transplant capability yet expose NO doctor count, NO bed capacity, and no recent page update. Treat as risk-to-verify, not proof of misrepresentation — but never send a patient on an unverified advanced claim.

props.claim: 1,219 facilities (29.9% of advanced-claimers) advertise advanced care with zero hard corroboration.
props.evidence: advanced-claimers 4,077 (40.8%); unverifiable (claim AND no doctors AND no capacity AND stale) 1,219; 37.8% of claimers have no hard proof
props.why: Self-reported scraped claims with no corroboration are the highest patient-safety risk in a referral.
props.window: facilities sample, India; word-boundary capability regex

nodes.name: the-data-readiness-tax
nodes.content: Before a planner can trust any number, the data needs work: 88 junk rows + 11 duplicate IDs in facilities; missing encoded as the string 'null' (so numberDoctors is real for 36%, not 99.7%); 49 NFHS columns are string-numeric with 26.6% of cells suppressed ('*') or low-sample estimates; 8 district names collide across states; the PIN directory fans out ~8x. None of it is optional.

props.claim: Trusting this data requires fixing string-'null' sentinels, 49 CAST columns, 26.6% suppressed cells, PIN fan-out, and 8 colliding district names.
props.evidence: facilities: 88 junk, 11 dup ids, 'null' sentinel (doctors 99.7%->36%); NFHS: 49 string cols, 9,193/34,594 cells (26.6%) suppressed/estimated; PIN: 8.5 rows/PIN, 1,478 multi-district
props.why: Every desert/trust claim is only as honest as the cleaning behind it — the 'communicate uncertainty' rubric.

nodes.name: geocoding-ready-to-map
nodes.content: The good news: 99.7% of facilities have clean lat/lon (99.64% inside India's bbox) and 97.7% carry a parseable 6-digit PIN, so facilities map directly with no geocoding step — the supply layer is map-ready even where the demand join needs name normalization.

props.claim: 99.7% of facilities are geocoded and 97.7% have a parseable PIN — the supply layer is map-ready.
props.why: Clean coordinates let a planner dot-map supply against the burden choropleth immediately.

nodes.name: Where are India's medical deserts (high need, low coverage)?
nodes.content: The flagship Medical Desert Planner question. Joins NFHS burden to in-dataset facility coverage to rank high-need low-coverage districts.

nodes.name: Which districts have zero facilities in the dataset?
nodes.content: The coverage-gap headline: ~245 of 698 NFHS districts have no facility in this sample.

nodes.name: Does facility supply follow health need across India?
nodes.content: The analytical twist: burden vs coverage correlation (negative, supply does not follow need).

nodes.name: Which districts have the highest health burden?
nodes.content: Demand-side ranking via the HBI composite (Araria 89.7; 10 of 15 worst in Bihar).

nodes.name: Which states have the best and worst health outcomes?
nodes.content: State HBI rollup: Bihar 78.4 worst vs Kerala 16.9 best.

nodes.name: How unequal is healthcare access across India?
nodes.content: The care-lottery spread: up to 81.5x district variation in insurance, 22.4x in 4+ANC.

nodes.name: Where is anaemia / child malnutrition worst?
nodes.content: Single-indicator district ranking (women anaemia median 57%, stunting peaks 60.6%).

nodes.name: Can I trust this facility's claims?
nodes.content: Facility trust score over 8 corroboration signals; cite the source text field.

nodes.name: Which facilities claim advanced care they can't back up?
nodes.content: Unverifiable advanced-claim filter (1,219 facilities; MRI/CT/ICU/cancer with no proof).

nodes.name: How concentrated is facility coverage?
nodes.content: Gini + top-k share across states (0.609) and districts (0.821).

nodes.name: What kinds of facilities are in the dataset and who runs them?
nodes.content: Composition: hospital 56% / clinic 38%; 88% private.

nodes.name: What must be fixed before I can trust this data?
nodes.content: The data-readiness punch list: string-'null' sentinels, 49 CAST cols, 26.6% suppressed NFHS cells, PIN fan-out, colliding district names.

nodes.name: How do facilities map to districts?
nodes.content: The PIN-crosswalk join methodology (facility PIN -> modal district -> NFHS).

nodes.name: Brief me on a district
nodes.content: District profile: burden indicators + dataset facility coverage + trust caveat.

nodes.name: Build a data story / infographic on India's medical deserts
nodes.content: Produce the 'Care Lottery' scrollytelling/infographic spine (burden choropleth -> deserts -> lottery -> supply-vs-need -> trust -> close).

nodes.name: Build a deck on the medical desert findings
nodes.content: Produce a PPTX deck from the verified desert findings.

nodes.name: Map facility supply against district health burden
nodes.content: Supply points (dot layer) over a demand choropleth + the supply-vs-need bubble.

nodes.name: Choropleth shows rates, not totals
nodes.content: On a district map fill polygons with a RATE/PREVALENCE (e.g. % child anaemia), never a raw count or facility count — a count choropleth mostly visualises the size of the geographic unit. Use proportional symbols for any magnitude. [[choropleth-map]]

props.statement: On a district map fill polygons with a RATE/PREVALENCE (e.g. % child anaemia), never a raw count or facility count — a count choropleth mostly visualises the size of the geographic unit. Use proportional symbols for any magnitude. [[choropleth-map]]

nodes.name: Sequential ramp for burden; diverging only at a real midpoint
nodes.content: Use a single-hue SEQUENTIAL ramp for an ordered burden measure (lightness is perceptually ordered); reserve a two-hue DIVERGING ramp for measures that cross a meaningful midpoint (need-vs-supply z-score around 0). Never rainbow. [[colour-encoding]]

props.statement: Use a single-hue SEQUENTIAL ramp for an ordered burden measure (lightness is perceptually ordered); reserve a two-hue DIVERGING ramp for measures that cross a meaningful midpoint (need-vs-supply z-score around 0). Never rainbow. [[colour-encoding]]

nodes.name: Facility points are a dot/proportional-symbol layer, not a fill
nodes.content: Render the ~10k facilities as a POINT layer over the demand choropleth, visually distinct from the fill underneath — never conflate supply count with demand shading. [[choropleth-map]] Spatial tab

props.statement: Render the ~10k facilities as a POINT layer over the demand choropleth, visually distinct from the fill underneath — never conflate supply count with demand shading. [[choropleth-map]] Spatial tab

nodes.name: MAUP & ecological fallacy at the district grain
nodes.content: Aggregating to district is a Modifiable-Areal-Unit choice: a pattern can weaken or reverse at a finer grain, and a district average does NOT describe any individual within it. State every claim as 'this district, at this grain'. [[simpsons-paradox]] [[confounding]]

props.statement: Aggregating to district is a Modifiable-Areal-Unit choice: a pattern can weaken or reverse at a finer grain, and a district average does NOT describe any individual within it. State every claim as 'this district, at this grain'. [[simpsons-paradox]] [[confounding]]

nodes.name: Always show the denominator
nodes.content: Never map or rank a facility count or a burden alone — pair it with its base geography and survey size. The 3 tables carry NO population, so frame supply as count-vs-burden, with NFHS women_15_49_interviewed only as a caveated survey-size proxy. [[choropleth-map]]

props.statement: Never map or rank a facility count or a burden alone — pair it with its base geography and survey size. The 3 tables carry NO population, so frame supply as count-vs-burden, with NFHS women_15_49_interviewed only as a caveated survey-size proxy. [[choropleth-map]]

nodes.name: Render suppressed and estimated cells honestly
nodes.content: NFHS '*' suppressed cells render as an explicit DISTINCT category (hatched 'data suppressed'), never as 0 or the lightest ramp colour; flag parenthesized '(x.x)' estimates (25-49 cases) as low-reliability. [[confidence-interval]]

props.statement: NFHS '*' suppressed cells render as an explicit DISTINCT category (hatched 'data suppressed'), never as 0 or the lightest ramp colour; flag parenthesized '(x.x)' estimates (25-49 cases) as low-reliability. [[confidence-interval]]

nodes.name: Sample, not census — label coverage
nodes.content: The facilities table is a ~10k SAMPLE (India has 47k+ in the FDR), so a low count is DATASET COVERAGE, not absolute scarcity. Label every desert/gap claim 'in-dataset coverage' and answer 'who is NOT in this sample?'. [[sampling-bias]]

props.statement: The facilities table is a ~10k SAMPLE (India has 47k+ in the FDR), so a low count is DATASET COVERAGE, not absolute scarcity. Label every desert/gap claim 'in-dataset coverage' and answer 'who is NOT in this sample?'. [[sampling-bias]]

nodes.name: Volume-filter rate maps to kill small-sample extremes
nodes.content: A district can show an extreme burden or perfect ratio purely on a tiny sample; pair any rate map or ranked bar with a minimum-volume filter or annotate n. [[choropleth-map]] [[confidence-interval]]

props.statement: A district can show an extreme burden or perfect ratio purely on a tiny sample; pair any rate map or ranked bar with a minimum-volume filter or annotate n. [[choropleth-map]] [[confidence-interval]]

nodes.name: Small multiples for many indicators on a shared scale
nodes.content: With 100+ NFHS indicators, use small multiples / a Trellis of identically-scaled mini-maps or ranked bars (same colour breaks, same axis across panels), never one multi-hue map. [[no-unjustified-3d]] [[sensible-scales]]

props.statement: With 100+ NFHS indicators, use small multiples / a Trellis of identically-scaled mini-maps or ranked bars (same colour breaks, same axis across panels), never one multi-hue map. [[no-unjustified-3d]] [[sensible-scales]]

nodes.name: Pair a burden choropleth with a companion ranked bar
nodes.content: Colour saturation is rank 5/5 in the Cleveland-McGill hierarchy — readers can't decode precise values from a shade — so always pair the map (WHERE) with a ranked bar of the worst districts (HOW MUCH, position/length). [[cleveland-mcgill-encoding-hierarchy]]

props.statement: Colour saturation is rank 5/5 in the Cleveland-McGill hierarchy — readers can't decode precise values from a shade — so always pair the map (WHERE) with a ranked bar of the worst districts (HOW MUCH, position/length). [[cleveland-mcgill-encoding-hierarchy]]

nodes.name: State the choropleth class-binning and show breakpoints
nodes.content: Class breaks change the story: quantile bins even out counts but hide magnitude; equal-interval preserves magnitude but can empty bins under skew. State which binning you used, show breakpoints in the legend, keep the same breaks across small multiples. [[sensible-scales]]

props.statement: Class breaks change the story: quantile bins even out counts but hide magnitude; equal-interval preserves magnitude but can empty bins under skew. State which binning you used, show breakpoints in the legend, keep the same breaks across small multiples. [[sensible-scales]]

nodes.name: Supply-vs-need bubble needs a reference line
nodes.content: On the need-vs-coverage bubble (x=burden, y=coverage) add the expected/diagonal reference line and label the high-need-low-coverage quadrant as the 'medical desert' region — the eye reads position-to-a-reference far better than a bare cloud. [[scatter-plot]]

props.statement: On the need-vs-coverage bubble (x=burden, y=coverage) add the expected/diagonal reference line and label the high-need-low-coverage quadrant as the 'medical desert' region — the eye reads position-to-a-reference far better than a bare cloud. [[scatter-plot]]

nodes.name: Hunt confounders before calling a district a desert
nodes.content: A low in-dataset count can be driven by data-collection coverage, urban/rural split, or private/public mix rather than true absence of care — name the lurking variable and scope the claim. [[confounding]] [[spurious-correlation]]

props.statement: A low in-dataset count can be driven by data-collection coverage, urban/rural split, or private/public mix rather than true absence of care — name the lurking variable and scope the claim. [[confounding]] [[spurious-correlation]]

nodes.name: Human-anchor device — carry one district through the story
nodes.content: Carry ONE real district as the human anchor — Sukma, Chhattisgarh (~91% children anaemic, ZERO dataset facilities) — open on it, return to it in the desert/lottery/closing scenes, contrast against Chennai (55%, 272 facilities). The reader follows a place, not a table.

props.statement: Carry ONE real district as the human anchor — Sukma, Chhattisgarh (~91% children anaemic, ZERO dataset facilities) — open on it, return to it in the desert/lottery/closing scenes, contrast against Chennai (55%, 272 facilities). The reader follows a place, not a table.

nodes.name: Lead the bubble scene with the expectation, then break it
nodes.content: The analytical payload is the reversal: you'd expect facilities to follow burden, but they don't (rho ~ -0.2; high-burden districts average fewer facilities). State the expectation first, then break it; claim 'mismatch between need and coverage', never 'discrimination'.

props.statement: The analytical payload is the reversal: you'd expect facilities to follow burden, but they don't (rho ~ -0.2; high-burden districts average fewer facilities). State the expectation first, then break it; claim 'mismatch between need and coverage', never 'discrimination'.

nodes.name: Split 'claimed' vs 'proven' in the trust scene
nodes.content: Visually split what facilities CLAIM (specialties/equipment ~99%) from what's PROVEN (doctors 36%, capacity 25%) so a planner never sends a patient on an unverified claim; cite the source TEXT field for any specific facility claim.

props.statement: Visually split what facilities CLAIM (specialties/equipment ~99%) from what's PROVEN (doctors 36%, capacity 25%) so a planner never sends a patient on an unverified claim; cite the source TEXT field for any specific facility claim.

props.via: normalized district (modal per PIN) = nfhs district_name
